result.markdown:
--- title: "Session 2: R Orientation" subtitle: "Getting comfortable with R and RStudio — just enough to follow along" format: html: toc: true code-fold: show code-tools: true --- ## What This Session Is (and Isn't) This session is **not** an R programming course. You do not need to memorise anything here. The goal is to build enough familiarity that when you see R code in the following sessions, you can: - Recognise what it is doing - Know where to change an input value - Run it yourself and see the output - Not be intimidated by it Think of this as learning to read a new language well enough to follow a conversation — not to write poetry. ## The RStudio Interface When you open RStudio, you see four panels: - **Source (top-left):** Where you write and edit code files (.R, .qmd) - **Console (bottom-left):** Where code actually runs and output appears - **Environment (top-right):** Shows variables and data currently in memory - **Files/Plots/Help (bottom-right):** File browser, plot viewer, package help ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(DiagrammeR) grViz(" digraph rstudio { graph [bgcolor='transparent', fontname='Helvetica'] node [shape=box, style='filled', fontname='Helvetica', fontsize=11] subgraph cluster_top { label='Top Row' style=invis source [label='SOURCE EDITOR\n(Write & edit code)\n.R and .qmd files', fillcolor='#4e79a7', fontcolor='white', width=3] env [label='ENVIRONMENT\n(Variables in memory)\nData you have created', fillcolor='#59a14f', fontcolor='white', width=3] } subgraph cluster_bottom { label='Bottom Row' style=invis console [label='CONSOLE\n(Run code here)\nSee text output', fillcolor='#f28e2b', fontcolor='white', width=3] output [label='FILES / PLOTS / HELP\n(See visualisations)\nBrowse files', fillcolor='#e15759', fontcolor='white', width=3] } source -> console [style=invis] env -> output [style=invis] } ") ``` ::: {#fig-rstudio-layout .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` The four panels of RStudio ::: ::: For this workshop, you will mostly: 1. Open a `.qmd` file in the Source panel 2. Run code chunks by clicking the green "play" button 3. See results in the Console or Plots panel ## Running Your First Code Let us make sure everything works. Run this code chunk: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # This is a comment — R ignores everything after # # Comments explain what the code does message("Welcome to R for HTA!") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` Welcome to R for HTA! ``` ::: ::: If you see the message, your R installation is working. ## The Basics: Variables and Arithmetic In R, you store values using the assignment operator `<-` : ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Store some values drug_cost <- 5000 # Cost of the drug in ₹ admin_cost <- 800 # Administration cost in ₹ total_cost <- drug_cost + admin_cost # Print the result total_cost ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 5800 ``` ::: ::: When you see code like this in later sessions, you will know: a value is being stored under a name, and you can change that value by editing the number. ### Quick reference of common operations ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} 10 + 3 # Addition ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 13 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} 10 - 3 # Subtraction ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 7 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} 10 * 3 # Multiplication ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 30 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} 10 / 3 # Division ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 3.333333 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} 10 ^ 2 # Exponentiation (10 squared) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 100 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} sqrt(100) # Square root ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 10 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} round(3.14159, 2) # Round to 2 decimal places ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 3.14 ``` ::: ::: ## Vectors: Multiple Values at Once A vector is simply a collection of values. This is how R handles data efficiently: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Cost of five different treatments treatment_costs <- c(1200, 3400, 5600, 2300, 4100) # Basic operations on vectors mean(treatment_costs) # Average ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 3320 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} sum(treatment_costs) # Total ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 16600 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} min(treatment_costs) # Minimum ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 1200 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} max(treatment_costs) # Maximum ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 5600 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} length(treatment_costs) # How many values ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 5 ``` ::: ::: In HTA modelling, vectors are everywhere — transition probabilities, costs per cycle, utility values across health states. ## Data Frames: Tables in R If you are used to Excel spreadsheets, a **data frame** is the R equivalent of a table: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Create a simple data frame — like an Excel table interventions <- data.frame( strategy = c("Standard Care", "New Screening", "Enhanced Treatment"), cost = c(1200, 2500, 4800), qalys = c(0.78, 0.85, 0.91), source = c("Sharma et al. 2023", "Patel et al. 2024", "Gupta et al. 2022") ) # View it interventions ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` strategy cost qalys source 1 Standard Care 1200 0.78 Sharma et al. 2023 2 New Screening 2500 0.85 Patel et al. 2024 3 Enhanced Treatment 4800 0.91 Gupta et al. 2022 ``` ::: ::: You can access specific columns: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Access the cost column interventions$cost ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 1200 2500 4800 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} # Calculate mean cost mean(interventions$cost) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 2833.333 ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(ggplot2) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` Warning: package 'ggplot2' was built under R version 4.5.2 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(interventions, aes(x = qalys, y = cost, label = strategy)) + geom_point(size = 4, colour = "#4e79a7") + geom_text(vjust = -1.2, size = 3.5) + labs(x = "QALYs", y = "Cost (₹)", title = "Cost vs QALYs for Three Interventions", subtitle = "Your first cost-effectiveness plot in R") + expand_limits(y = c(0, 6000)) + theme_minimal() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Visualising the interventions data frame](r-orientation_files/figure-html/plot-interventions-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: ## Installing and Loading Packages R packages are like add-ons that extend what R can do. For HTA, key packages include `heemod`, `ggplot2`, `BCEA`, and others. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Install a package (only need to do this once) install.packages("ggplot2") # Load a package (need to do this each session) library(ggplot2) ``` ::: **Analogy:** Installing is like downloading an app. Loading is like opening it. In this workshop, we will tell you which packages to load at the start of each session. You do not need to find or choose packages yourself. ## Reading Code: A Practical Guide You will spend more time **reading** R code than writing it in this workshop. Here are patterns you will see repeatedly: ### Pattern 1: Defining model parameters ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Transition probabilities (from Agarwal et al. 2021) p_mild_to_moderate <- 0.15 # Annual probability of progression p_moderate_to_severe <- 0.10 p_severe_to_death <- 0.25 p_background_mortality <- 0.02 # Costs in ₹ (from PMJAY 2023 schedule) c_mild <- 5000 # Annual cost of managing mild disease c_moderate <- 25000 c_severe <- 150000 # Includes dialysis ``` ::: When you see this, you know: **these are the model inputs, and you can change these numbers.** ### Pattern 2: A loop that runs the model over cycles ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Simple example: accumulating costs over 10 cycles n_cycles <- 10 annual_cost <- 5000 discount_rate <- 0.03 total_discounted_cost <- 0 for (cycle in 1:n_cycles) { discounted <- annual_cost / (1 + discount_rate)^cycle total_discounted_cost <- total_discounted_cost + discounted cat("Cycle", cycle, ": Discounted cost = ₹", round(discounted, 0), "\n") } ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Cycle 1 : Discounted cost = ₹ 4854 Cycle 2 : Discounted cost = ₹ 4713 Cycle 3 : Discounted cost = ₹ 4576 Cycle 4 : Discounted cost = ₹ 4442 Cycle 5 : Discounted cost = ₹ 4313 Cycle 6 : Discounted cost = ₹ 4187 Cycle 7 : Discounted cost = ₹ 4065 Cycle 8 : Discounted cost = ₹ 3947 Cycle 9 : Discounted cost = ₹ 3832 Cycle 10 : Discounted cost = ₹ 3720 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} cat("\nTotal discounted cost over", n_cycles, "cycles: ₹", round(total_discounted_cost, 0), "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Total discounted cost over 10 cycles: ₹ 42651 ``` ::: ::: When you see a `for` loop, you know: **the model is repeating a calculation across time cycles.** You do not need to understand every line — just the logic. ### Pattern 3: Creating a plot ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(ggplot2) cycle_data <- data.frame( cycle = 1:10, discounted_cost = annual_cost / (1 + discount_rate)^(1:10) ) ggplot(cycle_data, aes(x = cycle, y = discounted_cost)) + geom_col(fill = "steelblue") + labs(x = "Year", y = "Discounted Cost (₹)", title = "Effect of Discounting on Annual Costs") + scale_x_continuous(breaks = 1:10) + theme_minimal() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Discounted annual costs over 10 years](r-orientation_files/figure-html/pattern-plot-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: When you see `ggplot`, you know: **a chart is being created.** The `labs()` part sets the labels, `aes()` maps data to axes. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} grViz(" digraph workflow { graph [rankdir=LR, bgcolor='transparent', fontname='Helvetica'] node [shape=box, style='filled,rounded', fontname='Helvetica', fontsize=12] A [label='READ\nthe code\n(understand logic)', fillcolor='#4e79a7', fontcolor='white'] B [label='MODIFY\na parameter\n(change a number)', fillcolor='#59a14f', fontcolor='white'] C [label='RUN\nthe chunk\n(click play)', fillcolor='#f28e2b', fontcolor='white'] D [label='INTERPRET\nthe output\n(what changed?)', fillcolor='#e15759', fontcolor='white'] A -> B -> C -> D D -> B [style=dashed, label='Try again', fontsize=9] } ") ``` ::: {#fig-workflow .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` The workshop workflow: Read, Modify, Run ::: ::: ## Working with .qmd Files All workshop materials are in **Quarto (.qmd)** files. These combine: - **Text** (explanations, like this paragraph) - **Code chunks** (the grey blocks with R code) - **Outputs** (results, tables, plots) To work with a .qmd file: 1. Open it in RStudio 2. Read the text explanations 3. Run code chunks one at a time (click the green play button ▶) 4. See the output appear below each chunk 5. Modify values and re-run to see what changes You can also click **"Render"** to produce a complete HTML document with all code, outputs, and text formatted together. ## What You Need to Remember Almost nothing from this session needs to be memorised. Instead, remember these three things: 1. **Values are stored with `<-`** and you can change them by editing the number 2. **Code runs top to bottom** — run chunks in order 3. **If something breaks, look at the error message** — it often tells you what went wrong (a missing package, a typo in a variable name, a file not found) Everything else, you can look up as needed — or ask an AI tool, as we will discuss in Session 7. ## Try It Yourself Before we move to Session 3, try modifying the code below. Change the costs and QALYs to see how the ICER changes: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # YOUR TASK: Change these values and re-run cost_A <- 3000 # Try changing this cost_B <- 1500 # Try changing this qaly_A <- 0.82 # Try changing this qaly_B <- 0.75 # Try changing this # ICER calculation (do not modify below this line) icer <- (cost_A - cost_B) / (qaly_A - qaly_B) cat("ICER = ₹", round(icer, 0), "per QALY gained\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` ICER = ₹ 21429 per QALY gained ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} # Is it cost-effective at a WTP of ₹1,00,000 per QALY? wtp <- 100000 if (icer < wtp) { cat("→ Cost-effective at a WTP threshold of ₹", format(wtp, big.mark = ","), "per QALY\n") } else { cat("→ NOT cost-effective at a WTP threshold of ₹", format(wtp, big.mark = ","), "per QALY\n") } ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` → Cost-effective at a WTP threshold of ₹ 1e+05 per QALY ``` ::: ::: If you managed to run this and change the result — you are ready for the rest of the workshop.
